stem: A committee of [[k]] people is to be chosen from a group of [[n]] people. In how many different ways can the committee be selected?
solution: Order does not matter, so the count is \( \binom{[[n]]}{[[k]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the number of ordered arrangements \( {}_{[[n]]}P_{[[k]]} \); dividing by \( [[k]]! \) is needed because order does not matter.
This counts sequences with repetition allowed, not distinct committees.
This uses one fewer person in the pool.
This uses one extra person in the pool.
This adjusts the correct count by the committee size for no valid reason.

stem: A committee of [[k]] people is to be chosen from a group of [[n]] people that includes a person named Alex. In how many ways can the committee be selected if Alex MUST be a member of the committee?
solution: Alex's seat is already filled, so choose the remaining \( [[k]]-1 \) members from the other \( [[n]]-1 \) people: \( \binom{[[n]]-1}{[[k]]-1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the requirement entirely and counts all committees.
This forgets to reduce the committee size after fixing Alex's seat.
This treats the remaining choices as ordered rather than unordered.
This forgets to remove Alex from the pool of people being chosen from.
This adjusts the correct count by the committee size for no valid reason.
This adjusts the correct count by the committee size for no valid reason.

stem: A committee of [[k]] people is to be chosen from a group of [[n]] people that includes a person named Sam, who refuses to serve. In how many ways can the committee be selected if Sam must NOT be a member of the committee?
solution: Remove Sam from the pool and choose all [[k]] members from the remaining \( [[n]]-1 \) people: \( \binom{[[n]]-1}{[[k]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the requirement entirely and counts all committees, including those with Sam.
This is the count of committees that DO include Sam, the complement of what was asked.
This is only the count of committees excluding Sam via a subtraction that double-corrects.
This removes one extra person from the pool beyond just Sam.
This adjusts the correct count by the committee size for no valid reason.
This adjusts the correct count by the committee size for no valid reason.

stem: A committee is to be formed with exactly [[i]] members chosen from a pool of [[m]] available men and exactly [[j]] members chosen from a pool of [[w]] available women. In how many ways can this committee be formed?
solution: The two selections are independent, so by the multiplication principle the count is \( \binom{[[m]]}{[[i]]}\binom{[[w]]}{[[j]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This pools the two groups together and chooses the total size, ignoring the separate quotas.
This adds the two counts instead of multiplying them.
This swaps which quota applies to which pool.
This adds an unrelated adjustment to the correct product.
This adjusts the correct count by [[i]] for no valid reason.
This adjusts the correct count by [[j]] for no valid reason.

stem: A committee of [[k]] people is to be chosen from a group of [[n]] people, and then one member of that committee is designated as chairperson. In how many ways can the committee and its chairperson be chosen together?
solution: Choose the committee in \( \binom{[[n]]}{[[k]]} \) ways, then choose which of its [[k]] members chairs it: \( \binom{[[n]]}{[[k]]}\cdot [[k]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to also designate a chairperson.
This over-counts by ordering the entire committee instead of just picking one chair.
This picks a chairperson from the whole pool before forming the committee, which double counts differently sized committees.
This chooses one fewer committee member before multiplying by the chair count.
This adjusts the correct count by the committee size for no valid reason.
This adjusts the correct count by the committee size for no valid reason.

stem: A convex polygon has [[n]] vertices, no three of which are collinear. How many diagonals does the polygon have? (A diagonal is a segment joining two non-adjacent vertices.)
solution: There are \( \binom{[[n]]}{2} \) segments joining pairs of vertices in total; removing the [[n]] sides of the polygon leaves \( \binom{[[n]]}{2} - [[n]] = [[answer]] \) diagonals.
distractor_rationales: This counts every segment between vertices, including the polygon's own sides.
This adds the sides back in instead of subtracting them.
This is an unrelated formula that does not match the diagonal count.
This resembles the correct diagonal formula \( \frac{n(n-3)}{2} \) but omits the division by 2.
This is off from the correct count by [[n]].
This is off from the correct count by [[n]].
